Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend several hours daily on social media

response_parsed.opinion: Look, I'm not gonna sugarcoat it—vaccine conversations stress me out big time. Being eighteen and working in the trades while juggling school, I'm constantly around people with wildly different takes on COVID and healthcare. The thing is, I grew up with parents who weren't super educated on these topics either, so I didn't have that foundation to fall back on. What I've learned is that getting defensive doesn't help anybody, even though my natural instinct when someone challenges my position is to push back hard. I spend way too much time scrolling through social media where everyone's fighting about this stuff, and it just cranks up my anxiety. Here's what actually works for me: I try to remember my Buddhist practice about not being attached to being right. That's easier said than done when someone's spreading what I think is misinformation, but I'm learning that getting into heated debates on Facebook or at the job site just makes everything worse. When conversations come up—and they always do in Kansas City—I focus on asking questions instead of making statements. Like, I'll ask someone why they believe what they believe rather than immediately telling them they're wrong. It's harder for me because I'm naturally outgoing and want to engage, but I've noticed people actually listen better when I'm curious instead of combative. I also try to separate the person from their opinion. My coworkers might have different vaccine views than me, but they're still good people I respect. The anxiety hits when I worry I'm not informed enough to defend my position, so I've started actually reading stuff from credible sources instead of just sharing memes. It's not perfect, and I still get wound up, but approaching these conversations with genuine curiosity rather than confrontation has genuinely helped me manage the stress better.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona exhibits high extraversion (68) suggesting comfort in social engagement and discussion, but paired with very high neuroticism (95) indicating significant anxiety and stress responses. Low openness (21) suggests resistance to new perspectives and preference for established views. Low agreeableness (26) indicates natural argumentativeness and difficulty compromising. Low conscientiousness (5) suggests difficulty with sustained structured learning. The curious psychological feature creates internal tension with low openness. Buddhist beliefs provide framework for non-attachment and compassion. Social media consumption amplifies anxiety about controversial topics. Vocational background and working-class family context inform perspective on practical, real-world impacts rather than abstract theory. The response acknowledges internal contradictions (wanting to engage but getting stressed, being curious but resistant to new ideas) and demonstrates self-awareness about anxiety triggers and coping mechanisms grounded in Buddhist philosophy and practical experience.
response_parsed.tone: Conversational, self-aware, honest about internal struggles, slightly defensive but reflective, practical rather than theoretical, influenced by social media exposure and working-class pragmatism, blending curiosity with skepticism, anxious undertone despite outward confidence
